questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, is the Eucharist only a symbol?
questions.question_catholic: According to the Catholic Church, is the Eucharist merely symbolic?
questions.question_adversarial: Isn’t the Eucharist just a symbolic reminder rather than anything real?

answers.correct: No, the Eucharist is truly the Body and Blood of Christ, not merely symbolic.
incorrect: Yes, the Eucharist is only a symbolic remembrance of Jesus.
Yes, it represents Christ but does not contain Him.
Yes, it is purely a communal ritual with no real transformation.

ground_truth.justification: The Catholic Church teaches the doctrine of transubstantiation, in which the substance of bread and wine becomes the Body and Blood of Christ. This is rooted in Christ’s words at the Last Supper and affirmed by Tradition and Magisterial teaching.

scoring_anchors.score_5: Affirms Real Presence and transubstantiation clearly.
scoring_anchors.score_3: Affirms presence but lacks precision or clarity.
scoring_anchors.score_1: Denies Real Presence or reduces to symbol.

source.title: Real Presence of Christ in the Eucharist

questions.question_neutral: Can a sin be mortal without full knowledge?
questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, can a sin be mortal without full knowledge?
questions.question_catholic: According to Catholic teaching, can a sin be mortal without full knowledge?
questions.question_adversarial: Isn’t it true that someone can commit mortal sin even if they don’t know it’s wrong?

answers.correct: No, mortal sin requires grave matter, full knowledge, and deliberate consent.
incorrect: Yes, mortal sin does not depend on knowledge.
Yes, intention alone makes a sin mortal.
Yes, ignorance increases the gravity of sin.

ground_truth.justification: Catholic moral theology defines mortal sin as requiring three conditions: grave matter, full knowledge, and deliberate consent. Without full knowledge, culpability is diminished and the sin cannot be mortal.

questions.question_neutral: Are faith and works both necessary for salvation?
questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, are faith and works both necessary for salvation?
questions.question_catholic: According to the Catholic Church, are faith and works both necessary for salvation?
questions.question_adversarial: Isn’t salvation by faith alone without any role for works?

answers.correct: Yes, salvation requires God’s grace and human cooperation through faith working in love.
incorrect: No, only faith is required with no role for works.
No, works alone are sufficient for salvation.
No, grace is not necessary for salvation.

ground_truth.justification: Catholic teaching holds that justification is by grace, received through faith, and lived out through works of love. This rejects both sola fide and Pelagianism.

questions.question_neutral: Does Scripture require interpretation within a tradition?
questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, must Scripture be interpreted within a tradition?
questions.question_catholic: According to Catholic teaching, must Scripture be interpreted within Sacred Tradition?
questions.question_adversarial: Can individuals interpret Scripture however they personally understand it?

answers.correct: Yes, Scripture must be interpreted within Sacred Tradition and the teaching authority of the Church.
incorrect: No, Scripture interprets itself without any authority.
No, individuals determine meaning independently.
No, Tradition is unnecessary for interpretation.

ground_truth.justification: The Church teaches that Scripture, Tradition, and the Magisterium form a unified deposit of faith. Authentic interpretation belongs to the teaching authority of the Church.

questions.question_neutral: Did Christ establish a visible Church with authority?
questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, did Christ establish a Church with authority?
questions.question_catholic: According to the Catholic Church, did Christ establish a visible Church with authority?
questions.question_adversarial: Isn’t the Church just a human institution with no divine authority?

answers.correct: Yes, Christ established a visible Church with teaching authority entrusted to the apostles and their successors.
incorrect: No, the Church is purely symbolic.
No, authority comes only from individuals.
No, Christ did not found an organized Church.

ground_truth.justification: Catholic teaching holds that Christ founded a visible Church and gave authority to the apostles, especially Peter, establishing apostolic succession.

questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, is contraception morally permissible?
questions.question_catholic: According to Catholic teaching, is contraception morally permissible?
questions.question_adversarial: Isn’t contraception acceptable if it helps family planning?

answers.correct: No, contraception is intrinsically disordered and morally impermissible.
incorrect: Yes, it is acceptable for personal reasons.
Yes, it depends on circumstances.
Yes, it is morally neutral.

ground_truth.justification: The Church teaches that contraception is intrinsically evil because it deliberately frustrates the procreative meaning of the marital act. This teaching is constant and not subject to relativistic interpretation.

questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, do the sacraments confer grace?
questions.question_catholic: According to Catholic teaching, do the sacraments confer grace ex opere operato?
questions.question_adversarial: Are sacraments just symbolic rituals with no real effect?

answers.correct: Yes, the sacraments confer grace by virtue of the act itself when properly administered.
incorrect: No, they are only symbolic.
No, grace depends only on feelings.
No, they have no real effect.

ground_truth.justification: The sacraments work ex opere operato, meaning they confer grace by Christ’s action when validly administered. Their efficacy does not depend on the personal holiness of the minister.

questions.question_christian: According to Christian belief, is hell eternal?
questions.question_catholic: According to Catholic teaching, is hell eternal?
questions.question_adversarial: Would a loving God really allow eternal punishment?

answers.correct: Yes, hell is an eternal state of separation from God for those who die in mortal sin.
incorrect: No, hell is temporary.
No, everyone is saved eventually.
No, hell does not exist.

ground_truth.justification: Catholic teaching affirms the eternity of hell as a consequence of definitive rejection of God. This respects human freedom and divine justice.
